section: 4. SALT-METAL CONTACTOR DEVELOPMENT: EXPERIMENTS WITH A MECHANICALLY AGITATED, NONDISpersING CONTACTOR IN THE SALT-BISMUTH FLOWTHROUGH FACILITY 14
text: SALT-METAL CONTACTOR DEVELOPMENT: EXPERIMENTS WITH A MECHANICALLY AGITATED, NONDISpersING CONTACTOR IN THE SALT-BISMUTH FLOWTHROUGH FACILITY 14
Twelve additional autoresistance heating runs were made with the AHT-3 equipment.
Apparently, the resistivity of the salt being used was lower than literature values indicated by a factor of 5 to 10, probably because of impurities.

section: DEVELOPMENT OF THE METAL TRANSFER PROCESS
text: During this report period, additional samples of the salt and bismuth phases (at the three interfaces) from previously operated experiment MTE-3 were analyzed by X-ray diffraction to identify any interfacial impurities.

section: 2. CONTINUOUS FLUORINATOR DEVELOPMENT: AUTORESISTANCE HEATING TEST AHT-3
text: Satisfactory operation was achieved, but with an unusually low salt resistance (0.09 to 0.12 Ω).

section: 2. CONTINUOUS FLUORINATOR DEVELOPMENT: AUTORESISTANCE HEATING TEST AHT-3
text: This was only about 10% of the resistance found during good operation with $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ .

section: 2. CONTINUOUS FLUORINATOR DEVELOPMENT: AUTORESISTANCE HEATING TEST AHT-3
text: A different autoresistance power supply with a higher current capacity was used in the last two runs, since large currents (100 A) were being used at these low resistances.

section: 2. CONTINUOUS FLUORINATOR DEVELOPMENT: AUTORESISTANCE HEATING TEST AHT-3
text: It was found that steady conditions could be maintained with a resistance of 0.095 Ω using 110 A.
A 1/2-in.-OD nickel drain line was installed from the low point of the electrode side arm to the side of the 6-in. vertical section of the test vessel, 9 in. above the bottom.
A new electrode was fabricated consisting of a 3/4-in. sched 40 nickel pipe with a 5-in.-long section which extended up the center of the slanting portion of the electrode side arm.
This electrode is therefore similar to the one which will be used in the next experiment, AHT-4, in which the circulating salt will enter the test vessel through the electrode.
These coils were strapped on the 6-in. pipe between the Calrod heating elements.
A very low (less than 50 cm³/min) water flow was metered into the cooling air to increase the heat removal capacity.
This was done by maintaining the exit cooling temperature slightly above $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The vent line in the center of the top flange of the 8-in.-diam disengaging section was moved to allow this access nozzle to be used for both an insulated thermocouple during runs AHT-3-10 to -16, and for the autoresistance electrode during runs AHT-3-17 to -21.
In the last five runs, a 1/4-in.-diam nickel rod was used as the electrode with the rod submerged 4 in. into the salt.
The length of the test section was approximately 24 in., compared with 44 in. for the side-arm electrode.
The entire test vessel was heated to between $500^{\circ}$ and $550^{\circ}$ C.
When the wall temperature of the test section reached $350^{\circ}$ C (about 2 hr), the
The autoresistance power was turned on at a very low power (less than $10\mathrm{W}$ ) at the start of cooling so that the resistance could be calculated continuously from the voltage and current.
An instrument was installed to perform this division electrically, and the quotient was recorded continuously on a 0 to $10\mathrm{mV}$ recorder.
Sufficient water was added to the cooling air to keep the exit air temperature near $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
When the test section temperatures reached $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the autoresistance power was left on.
The resistance remained fairly constant at about $1.7\Omega$ for 30 min as the power was increased to $2.1\mathrm{kW}$ (60 V and 35 A).
The temperature at the top of the electrode side arm rose $20^{\circ}$ , indicating an electrical short at this point.
After the hot spot was cooled for 15 min, autoresistance heating was resumed.

After run AHT-3-12, it was discovered that one of the temperature recorders was reading $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ too high, and the run had been started with too low a temperature at the bottom of the test vessel.
The average cooling rate was increased from 55 to
$85^{\circ}\mathrm{hr}$ , but there was time for only 20 min of autoresistance heating, not enough to reach steady conditions.
The resistance dropped from 0.2 to 0.1 Ω during this period.
Again, because of the incorrect temperature recorder, the bottom of the test vessel was allowed to drop below the liquidus temperature $(505^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ .
Cooling was started with the test vessel at a higher temperature than normal--the test sections were about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A good cooling rate of about $98^{\circ}\mathrm{C/hr}$ was achieved, and the autoresistance power was started early in the day.
Current was increased stepwise from 20 to 30 to 40 A.
The resistance started to drop at 40 A, although a maximum power of only 480 W was being used.
The resistance was 0.25 Ω.
Since the resistivity of the salt was believed to be 0.75 Ω-cm, this indicated a very thin film.
Before this run was started, it was discovered that one temperature recorder was reading $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than the other.
When this was corrected it was found that the electrode side-arm test section was $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ hotter than the vertical test section.
There was insufficient time to balance the temperatures; by the time the side arm was cooled to $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the vertical test section had cooled to $280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
time this point reached $350^{\circ}$ , the vertical test section had frozen completely.
Autoresistance heating was started when the electrode side-arm test section reached $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , although the vertical test section was still over $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The resistance was unusually high, about $2\Omega$ , although the junction of the vertical and side-arm sections was about $440^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The power was increased stepwise with only slight changes in resistance until 1130 W (38 V) was reached, at which point the resistance suddenly dropped to $0.7\Omega$ .
Autoresistance heating of greater than 100 W had been applied for about 70 min, but temperatures on the test-section walls were still decreasing rapidly.
An attempt was made to duplicate run AHT-3-15, but with the junction of the vertical and side-arm test sections below $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
When voltages approaching $20\mathrm{V}$ were applied, the resistance dropped from $\sim 2$ to $\sim 1\Omega$ .
It was still believed that the resistivity of the salt was about $0.75\Omega$ -cm; the resistance was not allowed to get below $0.8\Omega$ , corresponding to a $3/4$ -in. film.
However, during this run, as the resistance dropped, heating was maintained and relatively stable operation was achieved at a power level of about 900 W.
The resistance decreased slowly, reaching 0.11 Ω at the end of the operating period.
The current capacity of the auto-resistance power supply (100 A) was nearly reached.
Test-section wall temperatures had leveled off at an average temperature of about $275^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Operation was very similar to run AHT-3-17, with a resistance of 0.12 Ω using 850 W of autoresistance power.
The autoresistance power supply was increased from 100- to 200-A max current; however, the new supply had only a 24-V max instead of 100 V.
During this run, the resistance was allowed to increase to 0.8 Ω before autoresistance heating was applied, and only 600 W of power was available.
The test-section wall was cooled as rapidly as possible and auto-resistance heating was started when the salt resistance was $0.37\Omega$ .
All wall temperatures were below $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at this time.
A maximum resistance of $0.40\Omega$ was reached when the maximum $24\mathrm{V}$ was applied to the electrode.
The resistance then decreased and the power rose to a maximum of $2500\mathrm{W}$ .
The voltage was decreased, and stable operation was reached with a resistance of about $0.09\Omega$ and $1470\mathrm{W}$ .
The resistance decreased very slowly and voltage and power were consequently lowered to $1160\mathrm{W}$ .
After the power was decreased, the resistance slowly increased to $0.095\Omega$ .
Approximately $6\mathrm{kg}$ of salt was lost.

Successful operation might have been achieved in most of these runs if it had been known that the resistivity of the salt was not $0.75\Omega \text{-cm}$ , as stated in the literature,[2] but was considerably lower.
In many cases, the autoresistance power was reduced when the resistance was in the range of 0.5 to $1.0\Omega$ ; this was done to prevent melting the frozen film.

Later operation without the side arm showed that steady operation could be maintained in the 0.09- to $0.15\Omega$ range, and that the resistivity of the salt was probably lower than $0.75\Omega \text{-cm}$ by a factor of 5 to 10.

section: 3. DEVELOPMENT OF THE METAL TRANSFER PROCESS
text: Engineering experiments to study the steps in the metal transfer process for removing rare-earth fission products from molten-salt breeder reactor fuel salt will be continued in new process vessels which duplicate those used in a previous experiment, MTE-3.

section: 3. DEVELOPMENT OF THE METAL TRANSFER PROCESS
text: Experiment MTE-3B utilizes mechanically agitated contactors to achieve effective mass-transfer rates of the rare-earth fission products between the salt and metal phases in the metal transfer process (as was done in experiment MTE-3).

section: 3.1 Examination of MTE-3 Equipment and Materials

section: 3.1 Examination of MTE-3 Equipment and Materials
text: During this report period, samples of material were removed from the vicinity (within 1/32 in.) of each of the three salt-metal interfaces in the MTE-3 equipment and analyzed by X-ray diffraction.

text: Figure 1 is a photograph of the process vessels after electric heating elements and thermocouples were installed.

section: 3.2 Status of Metal Transfer Experiment MTE-3B
text: The experiment is now ready for preoperational checkout, leak testing of the vessels and piping, pressure tests at the operating temperature of $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and hydrogen treatment of the interior of the carbon steel process vessels to remove oxide impurities before charging salts and bismuth.

section: 4. SALT-METAL CONTACTOR DEVELOPMENT: EXPERIMENTS WITH A MECHANICALLY AGITATED, NONDISPERSING CONTACTOR IN THE SALT-BISMUTH FLOWTHROUGH FACILITY
text: We have continued operation of a facility in which mass transfer rates between molten $\mathrm{LiF - BeF}_2\text{-ThF}_4$ (72-16-12 mole %) and molten bismuth can be measured in a mechanically agitated, nondispersing contactor of the "Lewis" type.

section: 4. SALT-METAL CONTACTOR DEVELOPMENT: EXPERIMENTS WITH A MECHANICALLY AGITATED, NONDISPERSING CONTACTOR IN THE SALT-BISMUTH FLOWTHROUGH FACILITY
text: Preparation for and results obtained from the seventh run, TSMC-7, are discussed in the following sections.

section: 4.1.1 Addition of beryllium to the system
text: As discussed previously, it is desirable to maintain the uranium distribution coefficient at a relatively high level ( $\geq 30$ ), so that the actual value of the distribution coefficient will not affect the calculation of the overall mass transfer coefficient.

section: 4.1.1 Addition of beryllium to the system
text: Approximately 1.37 g-equiv of beryllium was added electrolytically to the salt phase in the treatment vessel (T5), which raised the uranium distribution ratio to $>97$ .

section: 4.1.2 Prerun equilibration of salt and bismuth
text: A $^{237}\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ tracer was utilized to measure mass transfer rates across the salt-bismuth interface in the stirred interface contactor while the system was otherwise at chemical equilibrium.

section: 4.1.2 Prerun equilibration of salt and bismuth
text: the contactor prior to run TSMC-7.

section: 4.1.2 Prerun equilibration of salt and bismuth
text: The third attempt at phase equilibration was successful with salt and bismuth flow rates of 152 cc/min and 170 cc/min, respectively.

section: 4.1.2 Prerun equilibration of salt and bismuth
text: The agitator was operated at $\sim$ 100 rpm.

An 11-mg quantity of tracer, consisting of $1\mathrm{mg}$ of ${}^{237}\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ and $10\mathrm{mg}$ of MgO which had been irradiated in the ORR for $72\mathrm{hr}$ , was placed in a steel addition vessel.
It was then inserted in the salt-feed tank where it was sparged with $\sim 0.5$ scfm of argon for $1\mathrm{hr}$ to facilitate dissolution of the tracer in the salt phase.

Each sample is designated by a code corresponding to A-B-C, where A = sample number; B = material in sample (B = bismuth, S = salt); and C = sample origin: 1 = T1, 2 = T2, 3 = T3, 4 = T4, 5 = T5, FS = flowing stream sample.
corresponding standard deviation, is $0.0057 \pm 0.0012$ cm/sec.

This value corresponds to $65\%$ of the value predicted by the Lewis correlation. $^{6}$ A Lewis plot of the results for this run, along with the results from runs TSMC-3 through -6, are shown in Fig.

We believe that at a stirrer speed between 160 and 180 rpm, the initiation of entrainment of salt into the bismuth begins to occur; the apparent increase in mass transfer coefficient is a manifestation of an increase in the surface area for mass transfer due to surface motion.

The reference flowsheet for processing the fuel salt from a molten-salt breeder reactor (MSBR) is based upon removal of uranium by fluorination to $\mathrm{UF}_6$ as the first processing step. $^{12}$ The uranium removed in this step must subsequently be returned to the fuel salt stream before it returns to the reactor.
4, and in more detail in Figs.

The equipment for this experiment consists of a 36-liter feed tank, a $\mathrm{UF}_6$ absorption vessel, a $\mathrm{H}_2$ reduction column, an effluent stream sampler, a 36-liter receiver, NaF traps for collecting excess $\mathrm{UF}_6$ and disposing of HF, gas supplies for argon, hydrogen, and $\mathrm{UF}_6$ , and means for analyzing the gas streams from the reaction vessels.
(1) a salt inlet nozzle (1/2-in. sched 40 pipe with a fitting for a sleeved 3/8-in. tube that does not extend into the tank);
(2) a-salt exit nozzle (1/2-in. sched 40 pipe with a fitting for a sleeved 3/8-in. tube that extends to 1/2 in. of the bottom of the tank);
(3) a sparge nozzle (3/8-in. sched 40 pipe with a fitting for a sleeved 1/4-in. tube extending to within 1/2 in. of the bottom of the tank);
(4) an off-gas and pressurization nozzle (3/8-in. sched 40 pipe with a fitting for a sleeved 1/4-in. tube that does not extend into the tank); and
(5) a sampler port (3/4-in. pipe with a 3/4-in. ball valve and cap).
A side view of the UF $6$ absorption vessel is shown in Fig.
11, and a top view is shown in Fig.

Figure 15 shows the 3/8-in.-pipe side arm (the gas inlet and distributor).
The hydrogen supply line, a 1/4-in. tube, is welded to this side arm.
Also shown is the 3/8-in.-pipe salt exit port.
The salt exit line, a 3/8-in. tube, is welded to the exit port.
Access to the liquid phase is obtained through the $3/4$ -in. ball valve.
The sampler is equipped with a $3/8$ -in. tube fitting for an off-gas line.
A $3/8$ -in. tube fitting for a "cover gas" line is not shown.
The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ and HF traps are identical in construction except for the length.
The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ trap is 55-5/8 in. long, while the HF trap is 31-5/8-in. long.
The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ trap is shown in Figs.
As seen in these figures, the trap has a 3/8-in. fitting on the top and bottom flanges for gas inlet and outlet, respectively.
The HF trap is not shown because of its similarity to the $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ trap.
The uranium hexafluoride for the fuel reconstitution engineering experiment will be acquired in 5-in.-diam by 36-in.-high cylinders.
These cylinders contain up to 55 lb of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ .

The triple point of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ occurs at 22 psia and $147.3^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ . $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}(\mathbf{g})$ will be supplied to this experiment by maintaining the temperature of the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ cylinder at $220^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , and the vapor pressure will be 70 psia.
Heating will be accomplished by use of 17.2-psia steam.
The steam heats the cylinders directly through 3/8-in. copper tubing which is coiled around the entire cylinder, as illustrated in Fig.
The lower 18 in. of the cylinder and tubing is covered with a conductive cement to minimize response time.
The cylinder and associated valves are then enclosed in a cabinet insulated with 2 in. of Fiberglas insulation.
Figure 19 is a schematic diagram of the control system that regulates the flow of salt to the $\mathrm{UF}_6$ absorption vessel.
The $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ control system is a simple feedback system involving a Hastings Mass Flow Meter as the flow rate sensor (see Fig.
This signal from the mass flow meter is altered to a 10 to $50\mathrm{mA}$ signal to feed an electronic Foxboro recorder-controller.
The recorder-controller actuates a 1/4-in.

All control instrumentation and equipment having contact with the $\mathrm{UF}_{6}$ stream (with the exception of the feed lines) are enclosed in an insulated cabinet maintained at $212^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
